Preface 
The Mathematics II Teaching Module for Form II is designed to help teachers prepare and deliver 
Competence-Based lessons with a comprehensive understanding of Mathematics concepts, 
fostering a deep appreciation for the role of Mathematics in everyday life and its applications in 
various fields. This module aims to assist teachers to help learners develop analytical thinking, 
problem-solving skills, and project based skills through a structured and progressive learning 
approach. This module prescribes on hands-on activities, inquiry-based learning experience, 
encouraging learners to explore, experiment, and engage in problem solving reasoning. 
 
This Mathematics II Teaching Module for Form 2 aims to help teachers create a stimulating and 
supportive learning environment where learners can develop a profound understanding of 
Mathematics. This module will help teachers to guide learners to grow intellectually and 
personally by preparing them for professions in Science, Technology, Engineering and 
Mathematics (STEM) as well as for higher education by encouraging curiosity, critical thinking, 
and practical skills. 
 
It is hoped that this module will inspire learners to explore the fascinating world of Mathematics 
and appreciate its significance in shaping the future. 
 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe, (PhD.) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services  
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

HOW TO USE THIS MODULE 
To effectively use this Mathematics II Teaching Module for Form 2: 
 Read and familiarise yourself with the module's content, learning activities, and assessment 
guides. 
 Plan your lessons in advance, using the module's suggested teaching and learning activities. 
 Use a variety of teaching methods, including demonstrations, discussions, group work, and 
hands-on activities. 
 Encourage active learning by asking open-ended questions, promoting critical thinking, and 
fostering problem-solving skills. 
 Assess learners learning regularly, using the module's suggested assessment strategies and 
tools. 
 Provide feedback and support to learners, helping them to identify areas for improvement 
and develop their skills. 
 Integrate technology into your teaching, using multimedia resources and interactive 
simulations to enhance learner engagement and understanding. 
 Monitor learners progress and adjust your teaching strategies as needed, to ensure that all 
learners meet the learning objectives 
By following these steps, you can effectively use this Mathematics II Teaching Module for Form 2 
to support your teaching and promote learning.

ix 
 
INTRODUCTION  
Mathematics, as a fundamental STEM subject, which plays an important role in shaping learners' 
understanding of the natural world. As learners transition from upper primary to secondary 
education, it is necessary to provide them with an all-inclusive and engaging introduction to 
Mathematics. Therefore, the Mathematics II Teaching Module for Form 2 is a comprehensive 
educational resource designed to support the teaching and learning of Mathematics for learners in 
their first year of secondary education. It covers fundamental concepts and principles of 
Mathematics, aligning with the 2024 Mathematics syllabus. The module includes topic and 
subtopic overviews, suggested teaching and learning materials, environment set-up, learning 
activities, and assessment guides. Additionally, it incorporates teaching methods and strategies 
like inquiry-based learning, problem-solving activities, group discussions, hands-on activities, 
multimedia resources, and interactive simulations. The aim is to provide a solid foundation in 
Mathematics, foster critical thinking and problem-solving skills, and inspire further studies in 
Mathematics. 
SUGGESTED TEACHING AND LEARNING MATERIALS 
The study of Mathematics content requires hands-on experiences, visual aids, and interactive 
resources to strengthen deep understanding and appreciation. To support the teaching and learning 
of Mathematics II in Form 2, the suggested teaching and learning materials are meant to: 
 Enhance learner engagement and motivation in Mathematics  
 Develop practical skills and inquiry based thinking 
 Promote critical thinking, problem-solving, and analytical skills 
 Support differentiated instruction and inclusive learning 
The suggested teaching and learning materials are either artificial or natural. By utilising these 
suggested teaching and learning materials, teachers can create a dynamic and supportive learning 
environment that promotes academic excellence, creativity, and scientific literacy in Mathematics 
II for Form 2 learners. 
LEARNING ENVIRONMENT SET UP 
To create an effective learning environment for teaching and learning Mathematics is important in 
deepening understanding of the concepts and application in real life context. The learning 
environment set-up aims to create a safe, inclusive, and engaging space. 
 Natural Environment: A natural learning environment is a setting where learners explore 
and learn naturally, often without explicit instruction or formal teaching, such as in school 
surroundings 
 Artificial Environment (Man-Made): Artificial learning environments are intentionally 
designed safe spaces, such as classrooms, laboratories, and libraries, designed for formal 
instruction, hands-on activities, games, and songs

x 
 
 Technological Learning Environment: Access educational apps, games, and software for 
learning, including game-based platforms, virtual platforms, and simulations, to engage 
learners and promote learning 
SAFETY IN THE LEARNING ENVIRONMENT 
Safety in the learning environment is a requirement in learning Mathematics, as learners are 
exposed to potential hazards during hands-on activities. Collaboration between teachers, and 
learners, is important to create a responsible learning environment that promotes scientific inquiry. 
Guidelines for maintaining a safe learning environment include, risk identification, personal 
protective equipment, emergency response plans, storage, disposal, and learner responsibilities. 
Prioritising safety minimises risks, prevents accidents, and ensures a positive learning experience. 
 
TEACHING METHODOLOGY 
The effective teaching methodologies in Mathematics II include: 
 Conducting projects: Demonstrate key principles and encourage curiosity among 
learners. 
 Collaborative learning: Pair learners to work together, promoting peer-to-peer teaching, 
discussion, and problem-solving. 
 Conceptual learning: Connect mathematical concepts to everyday life, industry, or 
current events, making learning relevant and meaningful. 
 Differentiated instructions: Tailor teaching to meet diverse learning styles, abilities, and 
interests of different learners. 
 Feedback and reflection: Encourage learners to reflect on their learning, providing 
constructive feedback to guide improvement. 
 Inquiry-based learning: Encourage learners to explore, investigate, and discover 
Mathematical concepts through hands-on activities.  
 Integration of technology: Use digital tools, simulations, and visualisations to enhance 
engagement, understanding, and analysis. 
 Problem-based learning: Present real-world problems or case studies or scenarios, 
requiring learners to apply Mathematical knowledge to develop solutions. 
 Project -based learning: Assign open-ended projects, allowing learners to design, 
conduct, and present research or applications of Mathematical concepts.

xi 
 
 
By implementing these methodologies, a teacher can create an engaging, inclusive, and effective 
Mathematics learning environment. 
 
LEARNING ACTIVITIES 
Learning activities are intentional educational experiences aimed at promoting learning, 
engagement, and achievement among learners. Facilitated by teachers, they help acquire new 
knowledge, skills, attitudes, and behaviour change. To create an inclusive environment, teachers 
should use a "hook" or problem posing or scenario or key question or case studies to introduce 
new learning activities in an interactive and interesting way. 
ICONS USED IN THIS MODULE  
This module utilises icons as visual symbols or graphics to represent instructions, enhancing the 
learning experience for learners. Icons categorise and organise instructions, making navigation 
easier for teachers.

TIME ALLOCATION 
The standard minimum learner-teacher contact time for Mathematics II at secondary school level 
is 4 hours per week, translating to Six (6) periods with at least two double periods per week. The 
duration for a single period is 40 minutes. The contact time at Secondary school level is planned 
in such a way as to give ample time for practical activities. 
ASSESSMENT  
Formative Assessments: To monitor learner progress, identify areas of improvement, and adjust 
instruction to meet learner needs. 
Quizzes: Regular quizzes to assess learners’ understanding of concepts. 
Tests: Periodic tests to evaluate learners’ knowledge and application of Mathematics concepts. 
Class Discussions: Observing learners’ participation and engagement in class discussions. 
Group Work: Assessing learners’ ability to work collaboratively and contribute to group tasks.

xii 
 
Projects: Evaluating learners’ ability to design, conduct, and present a Mathematics project. 
Presentations: Assessing learners’ ability to communicate Mathematics concepts and ideas 
effectively 
Summative Assessments: To evaluate learners’ learning at the end of a lesson, sub-topic, topic or 
term, and to provide a comprehensive picture of learner achievement. 
Unit Tests: Comprehensive tests to evaluate learners’ understanding of Mathematics concepts at 
the end of each unit. 
Mock Exams: Comprehensive exams to evaluate learners’ overall understanding of Mathematics 
concepts at the end of the Term. 
Practical Tasks: Assessing learners’ problem solving skills and techniques through practical 
exams. 
Project-Based Assessments: Evaluating learners’ ability to design, conduct, and present a 
Mathematics project.

TOPIC 4: ALGEBRA 
Introduction 
Overview: Algebra is a branch of Mathematics that uses letters and symbols to represent numbers 
and relationships between numbers. It helps us to represent unknown values using letters, write 
mathematical statements called expressions and equations, find unknown values by solving 
equations, and describe patterns and real-life situations mathematically. At Form 2 level, algebra 
builds on the foundational ideas introduced in Form 1 and focuses on helping learners understand 
and use Algebra as a tool for representing and solving real-life problems. Through learner-centred 
activities, discussions, and practical tasks, learners develop confidence in manipulating algebraic 
expressions and applying algebraic methods accurately.  
This topic has two sub-topics namely basic processes, and remainder and factor theorems.  
           
General Competences 
 Analytical Thinking: Break down word problems into algebraic statements, interpret 
algebraic expressions, identify relationship between variables, and compare different solution 
methods and check accuracy of results. 
 Collaboration: Work in groups to solve problems on algebra and compare solutions. 
 Communication: Explain algebraic processes clearly using Mathematical language.  
 Creativity and Innovation: Use Algebra to model real-life situations in different ways, create 
patterns and general rules using algebraic symbols, and apply algebra in unfamiliar or practical 
contexts such as finance, measurements, and patterns. 
 Critical Thinking: Justify answers logically and mathematically, detect errors in worked 
examples, correct them, and make informed decisions based on algebraic reasoning. 
 Digital Literacy: Use calculators or digital tools to check algebraic solutions. 
 Problem solving: Solve problems involving basic processes, remainder and factor theorems. 
 
Key Terms/Vocabulary: 
 Algebra: A branch of Mathematics that uses letters and symbols to represent numbers 
 Dividend: The polynomial being divided.

3 
 
 Divisor: The expression by which a polynomial is divided 
 Factor: An expression that divides the polynomial exactly (remainder = 0) 
 Linear factor: A factor of degree 1, usually written as: (𝑥−𝑎) 
 Polynomial: An algebraic expression made up of numbers and variables raised to whole 
number powers and connected by addition or subtraction. 
 Quadratic expression: This is a polynomial of degree 2. 
 Quotient: The result obtained after division (excluding the remainder) 
 Remainder: The value left after dividing one polynomial by another 
 Zero (Root): A value of x that makes P(x) = 0 
Sub-Topic 1: Basic Processes 
Introduction  
In Form 2, learners are expected to deal with basic processes of Algebra, such as factorising by 
grouping terms, quadratic expression, and difference of two squares. They are also expected to 
add, subtract, multiply and divide algebraic fractions, and simplify algebraic fractions using the 
Lowest Common Multiples (LCM) and factorisation. These basic processes help learners think 
logically, solve problems, and apply Mathematics to real-life situations. Algebra also provides a 
foundation for more advanced topics in Mathematics. 
Specific Competence: Apply basic processes of Algebra in different contexts 
Learning Activity 1:  Exploring patterns and relationships in relation to Algebra 
 
 
Introduction 
Algebra is the language we use to describe patterns and relationships in everyday life. From the 
number of tiles in a growing design, the cost of buying several items, or the distance travelled over 
time, patterns help us to predict what comes next. In this unit, it is important to let learners explore 
number and visual patterns, identify relationships between quantities, and express these 
relationships using algebraic expressions and simple formulae. By recognising how quantities 
change together, learners develop the foundation for understanding variables, equations, and 
functions. Through hands-on investigations, discussions, and real-life problem-solving, learners

4 
 
will move from observing patterns to representing them symbolically using letters and algebraic 
expressions.

Activity 1.1: Research on patterns and relationships in relation to Algebra 
 Artificial environment: Books with content on patterns and relationships in relation to 
Algebra or any other research materials. 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with internet connectivity, and a 
projector for presentations. 
What to do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Provide them with 
computers/phones/tablets or books with content on patterns and relationships in relation to 
Algebra. Ensure each learner participates in the activity. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to explore the patterns and relationships in relation to Algebra. Let 
them present their findings to the class. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, Presentation, 
ICT, and Research skills.

Learning Activity 2: Factorising by grouping terms, quadratic expressions, and 
difference of two squares 
Introduction 
Factorising by grouping terms, quadratic expressions, and the difference of two squares are 
important skills in Algebra because they help simplify expressions, solve equations, and develop 
deeper mathematical understanding. Mastering these techniques strengthens learners’ ability to 
recognise patterns and relationships, which are central to success in Algebra and higher-level 
mathematics. 
 Problem Posing: A digital safe will only open if you completely factor the expression 
shown on its screen. One day it showed the expression 𝑥3 + 3𝑥2 −4𝑥−12 on the 
screen. To open this safe the following procedure should be followed: 
1. Use factorisation by grouping terms, 
2. Obtain a quadratic expression factor, 
3. Then factor it further using the difference of two squares. 
Mr. Phiri has a challenge opening the digital safe. As learners of Mathematics II, help Mr. Phiri to 
open the digital safe. 
 
Activity 2.1: Factorising by grouping terms 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets containing questions to factorise by grouping terms 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with a video on factorisation by 
grouping terms

7 
 
What to do: Start with the problem posed. Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed 
abilities. Provide them with computers/phones/tablets with downloaded video on factorisation by 
grouping terms. Give them instructions on how to find and play the video on the gadgets provided. 
Thereafter, provide them with worksheets containing questions to be factorised by grouping terms. 
Ensure that every learner participates in group work activity.  
Task: Ask learners in groups to factorise the questions by grouping terms, and present their 
findings to the class. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, Digital 
literacy, and Presentation

Activity 2.3: Difference of two squares (Paper Folding Model) 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment.  
 Artificial Environment: Plain papers, scissors, ruler  
What to do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Give each group a plain 
paper and a scissors for cutting. Ensure that every learner participates in the activity, and provide 
support where necessary. Make sure that 𝑥 units is longer than 4 units. 
Task: Ask learners in their respective groups to draw a square of side x on a plain paper. Let them 
cut out a smaller square of side 4 units and calculate the large area = 𝑥2. Let them also calculate 
the small area = 16. Ask them to express the remaining area, and rearrange the pieces to form a 
rectangle. 
Content tip: Let learners discover 𝑥2 − 16 = (𝑥 − 4) (𝑥 + 4), and expose them to 
a variety of questions on factorisation using the difference of two squares. You can also 
expose learners to questions where they start by factorising by common factor and then 
difference of two squares. 
 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, and Critical thinking. 
 
Conceptualisation: Make sure that the concepts of factorisation by grouping terms, quadratic 
expressions, and difference of two squares come out clearly, and give brief notes on them.  
Synthesis: Connect factorising by grouping terms, quadratic expressions, and difference of two 
squares to real-life situations. 
Evaluation: Check if the learners can factorise expressions by grouping terms, quadratic 
expressions, and difference of two squares and relate the concepts to real-life situations. Use a 
checklist to check the understanding of learners.

Assessment: Give learners tasks to confirm that they can factorise expressions by 
grouping terms, quadratic expressions, and by difference of two squares, and apply 
them in real-life situations 
Learning Activity 3: Adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing algebraic 
fractions 
 
Introduction 
Algebraic fractions are fractions in which the numerator, the denominator, or both contain 
algebraic expressions instead of just numbers. Like numerical fractions, algebraic fractions can be 
added, subtracted, multiplied, and divided, but they require additional skills such as factorisation, 
identifying restrictions, and finding common denominators. Understanding operations on 
algebraic fractions help learners to simplify complex expressions, solve algebraic equations, model 
real-life situations (such as, rates, ratios, speed, density, financial formulas), and build a foundation 
for calculus and advanced algebra.

Learning Activity 4: Simplifying algebraic fractions using Lowest Common 
Multiples (LCM) and factorisation  
 
Introduction 
Simplifying algebraic fractions using Lowest Common Multiples (LCM) and factorisation helps 
learners transform complex expressions into simpler, more manageable forms. By mastering 
factorisation and understanding how to find the LCM, learners gain strong foundational skills 
necessary for solving equations, working with rational expressions, and tackling advanced algebra. 
This learning activity builds both procedural fluency and deep conceptual understanding, as well 
as essential skills for success in secondary Mathematics.

Expected Standard: Basic processes of algebra applied accurately in different contexts

Sub-Topic 2: Remainder and Factor Theorems 
Introduction 
The remainder and factor theorems are important concepts in Algebra because they help learners 
to understand the relationship between polynomial division and factorisation. These theorems 
provide a quicker and more meaningful way of determining the remainder when a polynomial is 
divided by a linear expression, and of checking whether a given expression is a factor of a 
polynomial. Through this topic, learners develop the ability to simplify polynomial work, factorise 
cubic expressions, and solve cubic as well as identical cubic equations in an efficient and logical 
manner. 
The study of the remainder and factor theorems also strengthens learners’ reasoning, analytical 
thinking, and problem-solving skills. It enables them to see patterns in algebraic expressions and 
apply algebraic ideas to both classroom and real-life situations. In this topic, learners will explore 
the remainder and factor theorems, use them to find quotient and remainder, factorise cubic 
expressions, solve cubic equations, and use ICT tools to enhance understanding. 
 
Learning Activity 5: Exploring the Remainder and Factor Theorems 
 
Introduction 
Instead of performing lengthy polynomial division every time, mathematicians use two powerful 
tools, namely the Remainder Theorem and the Factor Theorem. These theorems provide efficient 
shortcuts that connect algebraic division with simple substitution, thereby transforming a complex 
procedure into a quick calculation. This section explores how and why these theorems work, 
helping learners move from procedural understanding to conceptual mastery. 
 
Problem Posing: A factory machine produces packets in groups following a pattern 
given by the rule 𝑃(𝑥) = 𝑥3 −3𝑥2 + 2𝑥+ 4. One day the manager sets the machine 
to 𝑥= 2, but instead of producing perfect packets, some items are left over. How can 
we predict the leftover items without actually running the machine? Is there a faster 
way than dividing the whole expression?

Learning Activity 6: Using the theorem to find quotient and remainder (including 
long and synthetic division methods) 
 
Introduction 
Using the remainder and factor theorems to find the quotient and remainder of polynomials is 
important because it allows learners to analyse and simplify complex polynomial expressions

15 
 
efficiently without always relying on length calculations. By applying these theorems alongside 
long division or synthetic division, learners can quickly determine whether a linear binomial is a 
factor, identify the remainder, and compute the quotient, which is essential for solving polynomial 
equations, factorising higher degree polynomials, and checking solutions. This skill not only 
strengthens algebraic reasoning and procedural fluency but also provides a foundation for 
advanced Mathematics, such as calculus, curve sketching, and real-world modeling in physics, 
engineering, and economics where polynomials often represent relationships that need to be 
simplified and analysed efficiently. 
 
  Problem Posing: A factory models its daily production with the polynomial 
𝑃(𝑥) = 2𝑥4 −7𝑥3 + 5𝑥2 + 𝑎𝑥−6 
Where 𝑥 represents the number of machines operating, and 𝑎 is a constant. During 
testing, engineers discover that when the production model is divided by (𝑥−2), the 
remainder is 10. 
Questions 
(a) Use the remainder theorem to determine the value of 𝑎, 
(b) Using the value of 𝑎, divide 𝑃(𝑥) by (𝑥−2) using synthetic division,  
(c) State the quotient and remainder, 
(d) Verify the result using division algorithm. 
Activity 6.1: Discovering the Remainder theorem by long division relay

worksheets containing different polynomials. Ensure that each learner solves a part of the long 
division and passes it on to another learner of the same group until the final solution. The group 
that finds the final solution first, wins the relay. Provide support to learners where necessary. 
 
Task: Ask learners in groups to solve different polynomial division problems using long division 
method. Let them present their findings to the class.

leaner participates in the group activity.

Learning Activity 7: Factorising cubic expressions 
 
Introduction 
A cubic expression is a polynomial of degree 3, such as 𝑎𝑥3 + 𝑏𝑥2 + 𝑐𝑥+ 𝑑 where 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐 and 𝑑 
are constants and 𝑎≠0. Factorising cubic expressions is important because it allows learners to 
break down complex polynomials into simpler factors, making them easier to solve, analyse, and 
apply in real-life situations. By factorising cubic expressions, learners can identify roots or zeros 
of the polynomial, which is essential for solving equations, sketching graphs and understanding 
the behavior of functions. It also helps learners apply the remainder and factor theorem efficiently 
and connects directly to higher level topics like Calculus and Algebra, where finding maxima, 
minima, or point of intersection often requires solving cubic equations. It also prepares learners to 
model real-world situations in physics, engineering, and economics where relationships are often 
represented by cubic functions. 
 
Problem Posing: A teacher wants to arrange learners in a cubical seating plan. The 
total number of seats S is given by, 𝑆= 𝑥3 −4𝑥2 −7𝑥+ 10. Factorise the cubic 
function to find how many 𝑥 learners can sit in each row so that all seats are filled 
exactly.

19 
 
7𝑥+ 10). Ensure that all learners participate in group work activity, and provide support to 
learners where necessary.

1. Test possible values of x by trial-and-error method.  
2. Find the values of 𝑥 for which 𝑓(𝑥) = 0.  
3. Identify the factor using the Factor Theorem.  
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Critical 
thinking, and Problem-solving.

them brief notes.

check learners’ understanding.  
Assessment: Give learners tasks to confirm that they can factorise cubic expressions 
correctly and apply them in real-life situations.  
 
Learning Activity 8: Solving cubic equations 
 
Introduction 
A cubic equation is a polynomial of degree 3, such as 𝑎𝑥3 + 𝑏𝑥2 + 𝑐𝑥+ 𝑑= 0, where 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐 and 
𝑑 are constants and 𝑎≠0. Solving cubic equations is important because it equips learners with 
the ability to analyse and model real-world situations that involve non-linear relationships. Unlike 
linear or quadratic equations, cubic equations can describe more complex patterns, such as growth 
and decay, volume problems, and physical phenomena where the rate of change is not constant. 
Mastering cubic equations also helps learners identify roots and intercepts, which is essential for 
graphing functions, optimising quantities and understanding turning points in higher Mathematics.

all boxes are distributed perfectly without leftovers), find all the possible values of 𝑥.

Learning Activity 9: Solving identical cubic equations 
 
 
Introduction 
Solving identical cubic equations is important because it helps learners recognise patterns, check 
consistency, and confirm solutions in both mathematical and real-world contexts. Identical cubic

21 
 
equations often appear in repeated problems or applications, such as volume calculations, seating 
arrangements, or parking problems, where the same relationship is modeled in different scenarios. 
By solving them, learners develop accuracy, confidence, and efficiency in finding roots, 
understanding multiplicity of solutions, and applying the remainder and factor theorems. It also 
strengthens algebraic reasoning and problem-solving skills, enabling learners to handle higher 
degree polynomials in Calculus, Physics, Engineering, and Economics, where verifying identical 
models is crucial for accuracy and real-life decision making.

Key Points Recap: 
 Factorisation by grouping terms is a method used to factor algebraic expressions (usually 
with four terms). It is done by grouping terms into pairs, then factoring out the common 
factor from each pair, and factoring out the common binomial factor 
 The difference of two squares is a special algebraic identity: 𝑎2 −𝑏2 = (𝑎−𝑏)(𝑎+ 𝑏). It 
works only when both terms are perfect squares, and there is a minus (−) sign between 
them 
 Factorisation of algebraic expressions is the process of expressing algebraic expression as 
a product of two or more simpler expressions (called factors). It is the reverse of expansion. 
It helps in solving quadratic equations 
 An Algebraic fraction is a fraction that contains variables in the numerator, denominator, 
or both. These algebraic fractions can be added, subtracted, multiplied or divided. 
 Simplifying using factorisation involves factoring both the numerator and denominator 
completely, then cancel common factors 
 Remainder Theorem: If a polynomial 𝑓(𝑥) is divided by (𝑥−𝑎), then the remainder is 
given by 𝑓(𝑎) 
 Factor Theorem: (𝑥−𝑎) is a factor of 𝑓(𝑥) if and only if 𝑓(𝑎) = 0. 
 A cubic polynomial has degree 3

TOPIC 5: MATRICES 
Introduction 
Overview: Matrices are an important part of Algebra because they provide an organised way of 
arranging numbers, symbols, or expressions in rows and columns so that information can be 
represented, analysed, and manipulated efficiently. They are widely used in Mathematics and in 
everyday life to organise data such as school records, prices of goods, transport schedules, sports 
scores, election results, inventories, and timetables. Through Matrices, learners are able to compare 
related information, perform calculations in an orderly manner, and solve practical problems 
involving many quantities at once. 
The study of Matrices enables learners to develop logical thinking, accuracy, and problem-solving 
skills. It also helps them to appreciate how Mathematics is used to represent real-life situations in 
compact and meaningful ways. In practical contexts, matrices can be used in business, science,

29 
 
ICT, engineering, agriculture, and education. Mastery of matrices therefore strengthens analytical 
skills, mathematical reasoning, and the ability to apply mathematical ideas to everyday situations. 
In this topic, the sub-topic is Matrix Operations. 
General Competences 
 Analytical Thinking: Identify patterns, relationships, and structures in matrices and use 
them to solve problems. 
 Collaboration: Work effectively with others in pair and group activities involving matrix 
formation, manipulation, and interpretation. 
 Communication: Explain matrix concepts, notation, operations, and solutions clearly 
using appropriate mathematical language and symbols. 
 Creativity and Innovation: Apply matrices in representing real-life situations such as 
timetables, stock records, transport fares, and test scores. 
 Critical Thinking: Compare different matrix operations and determine the most suitable 
method for solving a given problem. 
 Digital Literacy: Use calculators, spreadsheets, and other digital tools to represent and 
compute matrix operations. 
 Problem Solving: Apply matrices to solve practical problems involving organised data 
and systems of equations. 
Key Terms/Words/Vocabulary 
 Matrix: A rectangular arrangement of numbers or symbols in rows and columns. 
 Row Matrix: A matrix with one row. 
 Column Matrix: A matrix with one column. 
 Square Matrix: A matrix with the same number of rows and columns. 
 Rectangular Matrix: A matrix in which the number of rows is different from the number 
of columns. 
 Zero Matrix: A matrix in which all entries are zero. 
 Equal Matrices: Matrices that have the same order and corresponding elements equal. 
 Diagonal Matrix: A square matrix in which all elements outside the main diagonal are 
zero.

30 
 
 Identity Matrix: A square matrix with 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere. 
 Order of a Matrix: The number of rows and columns in a matrix, written as 𝑚× 𝑛. 
 Single element matrix: This is a matrix with only one element. It is also called as a 1 × 1 
matrix. 
 Element/Entry: A number or symbol in a matrix. 
 Transpose of a Matrix: A new matrix formed by interchanging the rows and columns of 
a matrix. 
 Determinant: A numerical value associated with a square matrix, especially 2 × 2 or 3 ×
3, used in solving problems such as finding inverses. 
 Singular matrix: A matrix whose determinant is 0. 
 Inverse of a Matrix: A matrix which, when multiplied by the original matrix, gives the 
identity matrix. 
 Matrix Addition: Combining two matrices of the same order by adding corresponding 
elements. 
 Invertible Matrix:  A matrix with a determinant not equal to 0. It can also be defined as a 
matrix with an inverse. 
 Matrix Subtraction: Subtracting corresponding elements of matrices of the same order. 
 Scalar Multiplication: Multiplying every element of a matrix by the same number. 
 Matrix Multiplication: Combining two matrices according to the rule of rows by columns, 
where the number of columns in the first matrix equals the number of rows in the second. 
 System of Linear Equations: A set of two or more equations with common variables that 
can be solved simultaneously. 
Sub-topic: Matrix Operations 
Introduction 
Matrix operations help learners to organise, analyse, and manipulate data in a systematic way. In 
everyday life, people use tables and arrangements of numbers to represent information such as 
prices of goods, examination marks, transport routes, attendance records, and stock lists. Matrices 
make it possible to handle such information efficiently and to carry out operations such as addition, 
subtraction, multiplication, and solving equations in a structured way.

31 
 
Through this sub-topic, learners develop the ability to identify different types of matrices, 
determine their order, perform operations on them, calculate determinants, find inverses, and apply 
these ideas in practical contexts. This strengthens logical reasoning, computational skills, and the 
ability to solve real-life problems using organised numerical information. 
Specific Competence: Use matrix operations in day to day activities. 
Learning Activity 1: Exploring the concept of matrices 
 
Introduction 
Exploring the concept of matrices helps learners understand that numbers and data can be 
organised in rows and columns for easier interpretation and use. In many real-life situations such 
as recording test scores, shop prices, classroom attendance, transport charges, and sports results, 
information is naturally arranged in tabular form. This activity introduces learners to matrices as a 
mathematical way of representing such organised data. 
Problem Posing: A teacher records the Mathematics, English, and Science marks for 
four learners in a table. The head teacher wants the marks arranged in a simpler form 
so that they can easily compare the performance of learners in different subjects and 
carry out calculations later. 
Questions 
a) 
How can the information be arranged using rows and columns? 
b) 
What makes this arrangement a matrix? 
c) 
How can the entries in the arrangement be identified? 
d) 
Why is a matrix useful in such a situation? 
Activity 1.1: Research on the concept of matrices 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Textbooks, charts, tables, newspapers, record books, price lists, 
timetables, and worksheets containing organised data.

32 
 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with internet connectivity, and 
projector for displaying examples of matrices from real-life situations. 
What to do: Start with the problem posed. Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed 
abilities. Provide them with examples of organised information such as school timetables, class 
attendance sheets, sports score tables, or shop price lists. Guide learners to observe the arrangement 
of data into rows and columns and help them relate this arrangement to the concept of a matrix. 
Ask learners to carry out research on the concept of matrices using computers/phones/tablets and 
encourage them to discuss where they have seen such arrangements in daily life. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to identify examples of organised data from their environment and 
represent them as matrices. Let them carry out research on the concept of matrices and present 
their findings to the class. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, Creativity and 
Innovation, Problem solving, and Research skills. 
Activity 1.2: Arranging data into matrices 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Manila papers, markers, counters, and worksheets with tables of 
data. 
What to Do: Present learners with practical information such as prices of mealie meal, sugar, and 
cooking oil in different shops, or scores obtained by teams in a sports competition. Guide them to 
arrange the data into rows and columns and identify each entry by position. Help learners 
understand that each number has a location described by its row and column. 
Task: Let learners in groups arrange given sets of information into matrices and identify selected 
entries using row and column positions. Let them present their work to the class. 
Content Tip: Help learners understand that a matrix is not just any table, but a 
structured mathematical arrangement of entries in rows and columns. Emphasise that 
position matters, and that each entry can be identified by its location. Encourage learners 
to move from practical tables to symbolic notation gradually.

33 
 
Skills to be developed: Analytical Thinking, Collaboration, Communication, and 
Critical thinking 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that the concept of a Matrix as a rectangular arrangement of entries in 
rows and columns comes out clearly. Give brief notes and examples from daily life such as marks, 
prices, attendance, and timetables. 
Synthesis: Connect the concept of matrices to real-life situations such as school records, shop 
inventories, transport schedules, sports tables, and agricultural records. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can identify matrix arrangements correctly, distinguish rows 
from columns, and explain why matrices are useful in organising information. 
Assessment: Give learners practical and written tasks in which they organise data into 
matrices and identify entries by their positions. 
Learning Activity 2: Identifying different types of matrices (row, column, square, 
zero, rectangular, equal, diagonal, identity) 
Introduction 
Identifying different types of matrices helps learners classify matrices according to their structure. 
This enables them to recognise similarities and differences between matrices and to prepare for 
later operations. Learners come to understand that matrices may be row matrices, column matrices, 
square matrices, rectangular matrices, zero matrices, diagonal matrices, equal matrices, or identity 
matrices depending on their arrangement and entries. 
Problem Posing: A school has different records: one contains a single row of transport 
costs, another contains a single column of attendance numbers, another records test 
marks in a square arrangement, and another has only zeros because no books have yet 
been issued from the library. The teacher wants learners to classify these matrices 
correctly and it happens that you are among these learners. How can these matrices be 
grouped according to their form?

34 
 
Activity 2.1: Classifying matrices from examples 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Flash cards with matrices, and worksheets. 
What to do: Start with the problem posed. Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed 
abilities. Give each group flash cards containing different matrices. Guide learners to observe the 
arrangement of rows and columns and classify the matrices according to their properties. Offer 
help where necessary as learners compare different types of matrices. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to sort the matrices into categories such as row, column, square, 
rectangular, zero, diagonal, equal, and identity matrices. Let them explain their answers to the 
class. 
Content Tip: Emphasise that learners should first check the number of rows and 
columns before naming the type of matrix. Then they should look at special features 
such as whether all entries are zero or whether the main diagonal contains 1s with all 
other entries equal to zero. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, and 
Classification skills. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that learners understand the characteristics of each type of matrix 
clearly. 
Synthesis: Relate the classification of matrices to practical ways of organising information 
differently depending on the context. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can correctly classify matrices and justify their answers. 
Assessment: Provide learners with a mixed set of matrices and ask them to identify 
the type of each one.

35 
 
Learning Activity 3: Stating the order of matrices 
 
Introduction 
Stating the order of a matrix helps learners describe its size accurately and prepares them for 
performing Matrix operations correctly. Since many matrix operations depend on the order of the 
matrices involved, learners need to know how to count rows and columns correctly. This activity 
strengthens attention to detail and mathematical precision. 
Problem Posing: A learner writes a matrix from a classroom attendance record but is 
unsure of its order.  The class now wants to determine the correct way of its order. As a 
member of the class, help the class to state the correct order. 
Activity 3.1: Finding the order of matrices 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets, manila papers, markers, and charts with examples 
of matrices of different sizes. 
What to do: Place learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Provide each group 
with charts and guide them to count the rows and columns carefully. Let them write the order in 
the form 𝑚× 𝑛. Connect this activity to the problem posed 
Task: Ask learners in groups to determine the order of the given matrices and present their answers 
to the class. 
Content Tip: Emphasise that the order of a matrix is written as number of rows by 
number of columns, not the other way round. Give several examples until learners 
become confident. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, and Presentation. 
 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that learners clearly understand how to determine and write the order 
of a matrix.

36 
 
Synthesis: Connect the idea of stating order of a matrix to real-life data arrangement such as 
learners by subjects, shops by products, or days by activities. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can count rows and columns correctly and state the order 
accurately. 
Assessment: Give learners written exercises containing different matrices and ask 
them to state the order of each correctly. 
Learning Activity 4: Transposing matrices 
 
Introduction 
Transposing Matrices helps learners understand that the arrangement of entries in a Matrix can be 
changed systematically by interchanging rows and columns. This activity develops the ability of 
learners to observe structure and relationships within Matrices. It also helps them to appreciate 
how a Matrix can be represented in a different but related form without changing the actual entries. 
In practical situations, transposition can be used when reorganising information such as changing 
data recorded by days and subjects into subjects and days, or changing lists of items and prices 
into prices and items for easier comparison and analysis. 
 
Problem Posing: A class teacher records the marks of learners in three subjects using a 
matrix in which the rows represent learners and the columns represent subjects. The 
deputy head teacher wants the same information rearranged so that the rows represent 
subjects and the columns represent learners. 
Questions 
a) 
How can the original Matrix be changed to suit the new arrangement? 
b) 
What happens to the rows and columns during the change?

37 
 
Activity 4.1: Investigating the transpose of a Matrix 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Manila papers, markers, flash cards with Matrices, and charts 
showing rows and columns clearly. 
 Technological environment: Projector for displaying matrix examples and animations of 
row-column interchange, computer/tablet/phone with software such as Microsoft Excel, 
Google Sheets, and MATLAB or Python (with NumPy) for rotating rows and columns.  
What to do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Start with the problem 
posed. Provide each group with simple matrices written on flash cards. Guide learners to copy 
each Matrix and then interchange the rows and columns carefully. Ask learners to use 
computer/phone/tablet with software to rotate rows and columns and present their findings to the 
class using a projector for displaying animations. Let them compare the original Matrix with the 
new one and describe what has changed. Offer help where necessary as learners discuss the 
meaning of transposition and identify how entries move from one position to another. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to find the transpose of given matrices and present their findings to 
the class. Let them explain how the rows of the original Matrix become the columns of the new 
matrix. 
Content Tip: Help learners understand that to transpose a matrix means to interchange 
rows and columns. Emphasise that the first row of the original Matrix becomes the first 
column of the transpose, the second row becomes the second column, and so on. Let 
learners practice with small matrices first before moving to larger ones. Encourage them 
to trace the movement of entries carefully so that they develop confidence and accuracy. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, and Digital 
literacy. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that the concept of the transpose of a matrix comes out clearly. Give 
brief notes and examples showing how rows become columns and columns become rows.

38 
 
Synthesis: Relate transposition to real-life situations such as rearranging marks, attendance 
records, agricultural data, transport tables, and business records so that information can be viewed 
from a different perspective. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can transpose matrices correctly, explain the movement of 
entries clearly, and compare the original matrix with its transpose. 
Assessment: Give learners written and practical exercises in which they find the 
transpose of matrices and interpret the meaning of the transpose in real-life contexts. 
Learning Activity 5: Performing basic matrix operations on Matrices (addition, 
subtraction, scalar multiplication and multiplication up to order 𝟑× 𝟑) 
Introduction 
Performing basic Matrix operations helps learners use matrices in a practical and meaningful way. 
Once learners know how Matrices are formed and classified, they should be able to add, subtract, 
and multiply matrices by a scalar. They should also begin to understand Matrix multiplication as 
a structured operation based on rows and columns. These operations allow learners to combine 
and compare organised data, making Matrices useful in practical situations such as shop records, 
transport fare tables, school results, budgets, and inventories. 
Through this activity, learners strengthen computational accuracy, logical thinking, and problem-
solving skills. They also begin to appreciate the conditions under which certain operations are 
possible, especially that matrices must have the same order for addition and subtraction to be 
performed. 
Problem Posing: Two shops record the number of exercise books, pens, and rulers sold 
during two days. A teacher wants to combine the sales records, compare the difference 
in sales, and also calculate how the sales would look if every value were doubled during 
a promotion. In another case, the teacher wants learners to see how rows and columns 
can be used to multiply matrices in practical situations. 
Question 
How can you help the teacher combine the sales records, compare differences in sales, and 
calculate how the sales would look if the values doubled during a promotion?

39 
 
Activity 5.1: Performing matrix addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Manila papers, markers, flash cards with matrices, and 
worksheets 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with software which can be used 
for addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication of Matrices such as MATLAB where 
available. 
What to do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Provide each group with 
pairs of matrices of the same order and guide them to add and subtract corresponding elements. 
Then provide a scalar and ask learners to multiply each entry of the Matrix by that scalar. Provide 
the learners with computers/phones/tablets with software and ask them to confirm the solutions 
they found from the physical calculations. Let them discuss what happens to the values after each 
operation. Offer help where necessary as learners compare results and explain the meaning of the 
answers in context. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to perform addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication on given 
matrices and present their findings to the class. Let them explain each step and interpret the 
answers using the practical situations provided. 
Content Tip: Emphasise that matrix addition and subtraction can only be done when 
matrices have the same order, because corresponding elements must be combined. Let 
learners begin with simple examples and practical contexts before moving to abstract 
exercises. Reinforce accuracy and orderly working at every stage. In scalar 
multiplication, every entry is multiplied by the same number. Ensure that you give tasks 
with matrices up to 3 × 3. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, Digital 
literacy, and Problem solving.

40 
 
Activity 5.2: Exploring matrix multiplication 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets with compatible matrices, and charts showing row-
by-column multiplication 
What to Do: Organise learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Present learners 
with worksheets. Guide them to multiply matrices using the row-by-column rule. Help learners 
see that the number of columns in the first Matrix must be equal to the number of rows in the 
second Matrix. Encourage them to write each step clearly and compare Matrix multiplication with 
ordinary multiplication. 
Task: Let learners in groups multiply compatible Matrices and present their solutions to the class, 
showing clearly how each entry in the product Matrix is obtained. 
Content Tip: For matrix multiplication, learners should follow the row-by-column rule 
carefully and not assume that multiplication is done entry by entry. Let learners begin 
with simple examples and practical contexts before moving to abstract exercises. 
Further, ensure that the multiplication go up to  3 × 3 matrices. 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Critical 
thinking, and Problem solving. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that the concepts of matrix addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, 
and matrix multiplication come out clearly. Give brief notes and examples showing the conditions 
required for each operation and the correct method to use. 
Synthesis: Connect basic matrix operations to real-life situations such as combining sales records, 
comparing transport fares, adjusting prices, analysing school marks, and organising agricultural 
data. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can identify when matrix operations are possible, perform 
the operations accurately, and explain their answers clearly. 
Assessment: Give learners written and practical exercises requiring them to add, 
subtract, and multiply matrices by scalars, and to multiply compatible matrices.

41 
 
Include practical situations so that learners interpret at least some of their answers in 
context. 
Learning Activity 6: Calculating determinants of a 𝟐× 𝟐 and a 𝟑× 𝟑 matrix 
 
Introduction 
Calculating determinants of matrices helps learners to find important numerical values associated 
with square matrices. Determinants are useful in checking whether a matrix has an inverse and in 
solving systems of linear equations. Through this activity, learners develop accuracy, logical 
reasoning, and computational skills. They also begin to appreciate how determinants are applied 
in practical mathematical procedures and in solving real-life problems that can be represented 
using matrices. 
This activity helps learners move from understanding matrices as arrangements of data to using 
matrices as tools for analysis and problem solving. 
Problem Posing: A farmer keeps records of maize, fertiliser, and seed used in three 
planting seasons. He arranges the quantities in a square matrix so that he can compare 
usage across the seasons. How can he use the idea of determinants to make future 
production decisions? 
Activity 6.1: Calculating determinants of 𝟐× 𝟐 Matrices 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Flash cards with 2 × 2 Matrices, worksheets, and charts showing 
determinant rules. 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with software such as Wolfram, 
Symbolab, Mathos AI, Matrix Calculator, and Desmos where available. 
What to do: Start with the problem posed. Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed 
abilities. Provide each group with flash cards containing 2 × 2 Matrices and guide them to 
calculate the determinant by multiplying diagonally and subtracting the second product from the 
first. Ask them to use software to confirm the solutions found after the calculations. Let learners

42 
 
compare answers and discuss the steps used. Offer help where necessary as they practice more 
examples. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to calculate the determinants of given 2 × 2 Matrices and verify the 
solutions using software. Let them explain each step to the class. 
Skills to be developed: Collaboration, Communication, Critical thinking, Digital 
literacy, and Problem solving. 
Activity 6.2: Calculating determinants of 𝟑× 𝟑 Matrices 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets with 3 × 3 Matrices, manila paper, or flip charts, and 
markers. 
What to Do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Present them with 
worksheet and guide them through the procedures of finding the determinants.  Let them show 
each step carefully and encourage them to work systematically to avoid errors.  
Task: In groups, let learners calculate the determinants of given 3 × 3 Matrices. Ask them to 
present their solutions to the class, clearly showing all working. 
Content Tip: Emphasise that determinants are only found for square matrices. Ensure 
that you expose learners to variety of methods of solving determinants. Let learners be 
exposed to real-life situations where the concept of determinants is used. 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, and 
Problem solving. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that the idea of a determinant as a single numerical value associated 
with a square Matrix comes out clearly. Give brief notes for both 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 Matrices. 
Synthesis:  Connect the concept of determinant of 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 Matrices to real-life 
applications. 
Evaluation: Check whether learners can calculate determinants of 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 Matrices 
correctly, and explain the procedure they used through a checklist.

43 
 
Assessment: Give learners written and practical exercises requiring them to calculate 
determinants of 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 Matrices and explain the meaning of their results. 
Learning Activity 7: Using the concept of determinants to find the inverse of a 𝟐×
𝟐 and 𝟑× 𝟑 Matrix 
Introduction 
Finding inverse matrices using determinants helps learners understand how a square matrix can be 
reversed in a way that is similar to finding the reciprocal of a number. The inverse of a matrix is 
important in solving matrix equations and systems of linear equations. Through this activity, 
learners discover that not every square matrix has an inverse, and that the determinant helps to 
decide whether the inverse exists. 
This activity strengthens the computational ability of learners, attention to detail, and reasoning. It 
also helps them connect earlier concepts such as square matrices, determinants, and identity 
matrices. By working with inverse matrices, learners see how different areas of algebra come 
together to solve practical problems in an organised way. 
Problem Posing: A group of learners is given a matrix representing information from a 
real-life situation in which they were required to calculate the determinant and make 
decisions. As they begin the task, they discover that some matrices can be inverted while 
others cannot. The teacher challenges them to investigate the condition that determines 
whether a matrix has an inverse and to explain how the inverse can be found and used in 
solving the problem. Under what condition does the given matrix have an inverse? 
Activity 7.1: Finding the inverse of a 𝟐× 𝟐 matrix using determinants 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets with 2 × 2 matrices 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with software that can be used to 
find the inverse of a matrix such as Desmos, MATLAB, and Symbolab where available. 
What to Do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Start with the problem 
posed then present learners with 2 × 2 matrices whose determinants are non-zero. Guide them step

44 
 
by step to find the inverse by first calculating the determinant. Ask learners to confirm their 
solutions by using software such as Desmos, MATLAB, and Symbolab where available.  
Task: Let learners in groups find the inverse of given 2 × 2 matrices and present their solutions 
to the class. Let them clearly explain condition (s) under which an inverse can be calculated. 
Content Tip: Emphasise that only square matrices can have inverses, and even then, 
the determinant must not be zero. Help learners understand that the inverse of a 2 × 2 
matrix is found through a clear procedure involving the determinant and rearrangement 
of entries. Encourage learners to check their answers by multiplying the matrix by its 
inverse to see whether the identity matrix is obtained. This helps them build confidence 
and accuracy. 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Critical 
thinking, and Problem solving. 
Activity 7.2: Finding the inverse of a 𝟑× 𝟑 matrix using determinants 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets with 3 × 3 matrices 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tablets with software that can be used to 
find the inverse of a matrix such as Desmos, MATLAB, and Symbolab where available. 
What to Do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Present them with 3 × 3 
matrices whose determinants are non-zero. Guide them step by step to find the inverse by first 
calculating the determinant. Ask learners to confirm their solutions by using software such as 
Desmos, MATLAB, and Symbolab where available.  
Task: Let learners in groups find the inverse of given 3 × 3 matrices and present their solutions 
to the class. Let them clearly explain condition (s) under which an inverse can be calculated. 
Content Tip: Expose learners to a variety of methods where the concept of determinant 
is used to find the inverse of  3 × 3 matrices. Encourage learners to check their answers 
by multiplying the matrix by its inverse to see whether the identity matrix is obtained. 
This helps them build confidence and accuracy.

45 
 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Critical 
thinking, and Problem solving. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that the concept of using determinants to find the inverse comes out 
clearly. Give brief notes to consolidate learners’ understanding. 
Synthesis: Connect the concept of using determinants to find inverse of matrices to real-life 
situations.  
Evaluation: Use a checklist to check if learners can use the concept of determinants to find the 
inverse of matrices.  
Assessment: Give learners tasks requiring them to use determinants to find the inverse 
of matrices.  
 
Learning Activity 8: Proving matrices using Commutative, Associative and 
Distributive 
(CAD) 
laws 
and 
other 
matrix 
properties 
[𝒆𝒈. 
[𝑨𝑩]𝑻=
𝑩𝑻𝑨𝑻,   [𝑨−𝟏]−𝟏= 𝑨] 
Introduction 
Proving matrix properties helps learners to move beyond computation and develop a deeper 
understanding of how matrices behave under different operations. Through this activity, learners 
learn that matrix operations follow certain rules and patterns which can be demonstrated using 
examples and proofs. These properties help learners to work more confidently and accurately when 
adding, subtracting, or multiplying matrices. 
This activity also strengthens logical thinking, reasoning, and the ability to justify mathematical 
statements. By proving matrix properties, learners begin to appreciate that mathematics is not only 
about getting answers but also about explaining why methods work. Such understanding is 
important for further learning in algebra and for solving more complex problems involving 
Matrices.

46 
 
Problem Posing: While solving matrix problems, a learner observes that adding two 
matrices of the same order gives the same answer even when the positions of the two 
matrices are swapped. However, when the same learner multiplies two matrices, 
changing the order of multiplication gives a different answer. This makes the class 
wonder why addition behaves differently from multiplication when working with 
matrices. 
Question 
Why did the learner get the same answer during addition when the positions of matrices were 
swapped but a different answer when the order was changed in multiplication? 
Activity 8.1: Proving the Commutative, Associative and Distributive laws  
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets containing matrices of the same order, manila papers, 
or flip charts, and markers. 
What to do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Start with the problem 
posed, then provide each group with worksheets and ask them to perform matrix addition in 
different orders. Guide them to compare the results and deduce the commutative, associative and 
distributive laws. Let learners discuss what they observe and record their conclusions. 
Task: Ask learners in groups to verify properties such as 𝐴+ 𝐵= 𝐵+ 𝐴, (𝐴+ 𝐵) + 𝐶= 𝐴+
(𝐵+ 𝐶), and 𝑘(𝐴+ 𝐵) = 𝑘𝐴+ 𝑘𝐵 using numerical examples. Let them present their work to the 
class and explain each result. 
Content Tip: Ensure that learners can verify whether commutative, associative and 
distributive laws work for both addition and subtraction or just addition only. 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, Critical 
thinking, and Problem solving.

47 
 
Activity 8.2: Exploring properties of matrix multiplication 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets with compatible Matrices. 
What to Do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Provide learners with 
worksheets and guide them to compute products such as 𝐴𝐵 and 𝐵𝐴. Help them compare the 
answers and see that matrix multiplication is not commutative. Then guide learners to verify 
associative and distributive properties. Encourage them to discuss why some properties hold and 
why others do not. 
Task: Let learners in groups verify that 𝐴𝐵≠ 𝐵𝐴, 𝐴(𝐵+ 𝐶) = 𝐴𝐵+ 𝐴𝐶 and (𝐴𝐵)𝐶= 𝐴(𝐵𝐶) 
where possible. Let them present their findings to the class. 
Content Tip: Help learners understand that matrix addition behaves much like ordinary 
addition when the matrices have the same order, but matrix multiplication is different. 
Emphasise that while matrix multiplication is associative and distributive over addition, 
it is generally not commutative. Ensure that laws such as [𝐴𝐵]𝑇= 𝐵𝑇𝐴𝑇,   [𝐴−1]−1 =
𝐴],    [𝐴𝑇]𝑇= 𝐴, and  (𝐴+ 𝐵)𝑇= 𝐴𝑇+ 𝐵𝑇 are proved. Use simple numerical examples 
so that learners can discover the properties for themselves. Encourage them to explain 
every step clearly and not just state the results. 
Skills to be developed: Analytical thinking, Collaboration, Communication, and 
Problem solving. 
Conceptualisation: Ensure that learners prove the Commutative, Associative, and Distributive 
and other matrix properties clearly.  
Synthesis: Connect proving of the Commutative, Associative, and Distributive and other matrix 
properties to real-life situations.  
Evaluation: Check if learners can prove the Commutative, Associative, and Distributive and other 
matrix properties clearly. 
Assessment: Give learners tasks requiring them to prove the Commutative, 
Associative, and Distributive and other matrix properties.

48 
 
Learning Activity 9: Solving systems of linear equations in two and/ or three 
variables using Matrices 
Introduction 
Solving systems of linear equations using matrices helps learners apply matrix concepts to 
practical problem solving. Many real-life situations involve two or more unknown quantities that 
must be determined simultaneously. Such situations arise in business, farming, transport, science, 
and education. Matrices provide a clear and organised method for representing and solving such 
systems. 
Through this activity, learners connect earlier ideas such as matrix formation, determinants, 
inverse matrices, and matrix operations. This strengthens their understanding of algebra as a whole 
and helps them appreciate the usefulness of matrices in solving problems involving several 
unknowns at the same time. Learners also develop reasoning, interpretation, and problem-solving 
skills. 
Problem Posing: A shop sells books and pens all in packs. On one day, Chali got 2 packs 
of books and 3 packs of pens which gave a total cost of K56.00. On another day, Chali 
got 3 packs of books and 2 packs of pens which gave a total cost of K54.00. Chali wants 
to know the price of one pack of books and one pack of pens. Help Chali to know the 
price of one pack of books and one pack of pens. 
Activity 9.1: Solving systems of linear equations in two variables by matrix method 
Suggested teaching and learning materials to set up the learning environment 
 Artificial environment: Worksheets containing questions on systems of linear equations 
in two variables. 
 Technological environment: Computers/phones/tables with internet connectivity, or 
computers/phones/tablets with downloaded video on solving systems of linear equations 
in two variables by matrix method. 
What to Do: Put learners in small manageable groups of mixed abilities. Start with the problem 
posed then provide learners with computers/phones/tablets with internet connectivity. Also 
provide them with worksheets. Guide them to download the video. In case there is no internet

49 
 
connectivity, provide learners with computers/phones/tablets with downloaded videos. Ensure that 
each learner participates, and offer assistance where necessary. 
Task: Let learners in groups download and watch the video on solving systems of linear equations

 Exploring the concept of matrices 
 Identifying different types of matrices (row, column, square, zero, rectangular, equal, 
diagonal, identity) 
 Stating the order of the matrices 
 Transposing matrices 
 Performing basic matrix operation on Matrices (addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication 
and multiplication up to order 3 × 3) 
 Calculating determinant of a 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 matrix 
 Using the concept of determinants to find the inverse of a 2 × 2 and 3 × 3 matrix 
 Proving matrices using Commutative, Associative and Distributive (CAD) laws and other 
matrix properties [𝑒𝑔. [𝐴𝐵]𝑇= 𝐵𝑇𝐴𝑇,   [𝐴−1]−1 = 𝐴] 
 Solving systems of linear equations in two and/ or three variables using matrices 
 Using ICT tools and software to calculate matrix operations and the inverse 
Summary 
 Key points Recap 
 A matrix is a rectangular arrangement of numbers or symbols in rows and columns. 
 Matrices are used to organise, represent, and analyse information in everyday life. 
 The horizontal arrangement in a matrix is called a row, while the vertical arrangement is 
called a column. 
 The order of a matrix is written as the number of rows by the number of columns. 
 Types of matrices include row matrix, column matrix, square matrix, rectangular matrix, 
zero matrix, diagonal matrix, equal matrix, and identity matrix. 
 A row matrix has only one row, while a column matrix has only one column.

53 
 
 A square matrix has the same number of rows and columns, while a rectangular matrix has 
a different number of rows and columns. 
 A zero matrix has all its elements equal to zero. 
 A diagonal matrix is a square matrix in which all elements outside the main diagonal are 
zero. 
 An identity matrix is a square matrix with 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere. 
 Equal matrices have the same order and corresponding elements equal. 
 The transpose of a matrix is found by interchanging rows and columns. 
 Matrix addition and subtraction can only be performed on matrices of the same order. 
 Matrix addition and subtraction are carried out by adding or subtracting corresponding 
elements. 
 Scalar multiplication is done by multiplying every element of a matrix by the same number. 
 Matrix multiplication is carried out using the row by column rule. 
 Matrix multiplication is only possible when the number of columns in the first matrix is 
equal to the number of rows in the second matrix. 
 A determinant is a numerical value associated with a square matrix. 
 The determinant of a 2 × 2 matrix is found by multiplying diagonally and subtracting. 
 The determinant of a 3 × 3 matrix is found using a systematic expansion method. 
 Only square matrices can have determinants and inverses. 
 A matrix has an inverse only if its determinant is not zero. 
 The inverse of a matrix is a matrix which, when multiplied by the original matrix, gives 
the identity matrix. 
 Matrix properties help learners understand how matrices behave under addition, scalar 
multiplication, and multiplication. 
 Matrix addition is commutative and associative, while matrix multiplication is generally 
not commutative. 
 Matrix multiplication is associative and distributive over addition. 
 Systems of linear equations can be written in matrix form and solved using inverse 
matrices.

54 
 
 Matrices help learners develop problem-solving, critical thinking, reasoning, accuracy, and 
interpretation skills. 
 ICT tools such as calculators, spreadsheets, Excel, GeoGebra, and other mathematical 
software can be used to explore and check matrix operations.

TOPIC 6: VARIATION 
Introduction 
Overview: Variation in Mathematics describes the relationship between two quantities and how a 
change in one quantity affects the other. It helps learners understand how variables are connected 
and how mathematical models can represent real-life situations. In this topic, learners explore 
different types of relationships between variables such direct, inverse, joint, and partial variations. 
They learn how to express these relationships using mathematical equations, tables and graphs. 
For example, when two quantities increase or decrease together at a constant rate, they are said to 
vary directly. When one quantity increases while the other decreases, they vary inversely. 
Understanding variation helps learners apply Mathematics to real-life situations such as, speed and 
time relationships, cost and quantity relationships, pressure and volume in science, and other 
proportional relationships.  
Through this topic, learners develop skills in algebraic thinking, problem-solving, graph 
interpretation, and mathematical communication. 
In this topic, the sub-topic is types of variation. 
General Competences: 
 Analytical thinking: Identify how variables are related, comparing different types of 
variations, and predict how changes in one variable affects the other. 
 Collaboration: Discuss solutions, and working together to solve variation problems. 
 Communication: Explaining mathematical relationships 
 Creativity and innovation: Modelling real-life situations using variation equations and 
graphs. 
  Critical thinking: Analysing relationships between variables and determine the type or 
variation involved. 
 Digital literacy: Using calculators, graphing tools, or digital platforms to represent and 
analyse variations. 
 Problem solving: Solve problems involving different types of variations.

59 
 
Terms /Words/Vocabulary: 
 Variation: A relationship between two or more variables where a change in one affects 
the other. 
 Variable: A symbol (usually a letter) that represents a quantity that can change. 
 Constant of Variation: A fixed number that relates the variables in a variation equation. 
 Direct Variation: When two quantities increase or decrease together in the same 
proportion. 
 Inverse Variation: When one quantity increases while the other decreases proportionally. 
 Joint Variation: When one variable varies directly with two or more variables. 
 Partial Variation: A relationship between variables where one variable changes partly in 
direct proportion to another variable and partly as a constant (fixed amount). 
 Proportional Relationships: A relationship where two quantities maintain a constant 
ratio. 
 Equation of Variation: A mathematical expression that represents the relationship 
between variables. 
 Graph of Variation: A visual representation of the relationship between variables on a 
coordinate plane. 
Sub-topic: Types of Variation 
Introduction 
Types of variation help learners understand how quantities change in relation to each other. This 
knowledge is important for solving practical problems in everyday life and for developing strong 
mathematical skills. The main types of variation are direct, inverse, joint, and partial variations. 
These are widely used in real-life situations such as, wages and hours worked, speed and travel 
time, number of workers and time taken to complete a job, force in physics, intensity of light, and 
many others. 
Specific Competence: Apply the concept of variation in daily contexts.

60 
 
Learning Activity 1: Exploring related proportions in the environment such as 
age and height, water temperature and depth and so on 
Introduction 
In everyday life, many quantities are related and change together. Understanding how these 
quantities are connected helps learners make sense of the world around them. In mathematics, 
these relationships are often studied through proportions and variations, where one quantity 
changes depending on another. For example, as children grow older, their height generally 
increases, the temperature of water may change with depth, and the length of a shadow changes 
depending on the position of the sun during the day. Through exploring related proportions in the 
environment, learners develop important competences such as critical thinking, problem solving, 
collaboration, observation, and data interpretation. This approach not only strengthens their 
mathematical understanding but also helps them appreciate how mathematics is applied in daily 
life. 
Problem Posing: Why are older learners usually taller than younger ones? Does age 
always increase with height in the same proportion?

Learning Activity 3: Identifying types of Variations (Direct, Inverse, Joint, and 
Partial). 
Introduction 
At this level, four types of variations are covered, namely, direct variation, which shows 
proportional increase or decrease, Inverse variation, which shows opposite change between 
variables, Joint variation, which involves dependence on multipole variables, and Partial variation, 
which combines a constant value with a varying component. Understanding types of variation 
helps learners to recognise how quantities relate in real-life situations. Through investigation, 
experimentation, and real-world simulations learners can understand how variables relate, and that 
helps us to predict outcomes, model-real-world situations, and solve practical problems in fields 
such as science, economics, and engineering.
